raw_response:
{
  "internal_reasoning": "This painting is consistent in subject, palette, and compositional language with George Condo’s mature 2000s–2010s body of work, especially his highly sought-after ‘Expanded Portrait’ and multi-figure grotesque compositions that synthesize Old Master figuration, Picasso-derived fragmentation, cartoon distortion, and psychological caricature. The title, date, and image suggest a fully realized, market-appropriate Condo from a strong period. Around 2010, Condo was producing works that became central to his current market identity: inventive, theatrical, intellectually legible, and immediately recognizable. The present image shows multiple interlocking heads and bodies arranged across a square support, with fluid contour drawing over broad passages of cool blue, green, lavender, gray, and white. The composition has good visual density and a convincing rhythm of line, mass, and facial invention. The central and left figures carry the absurdist, grotesque physiognomies for which Condo is best known, while the right-hand female nude-like figure introduces a more lyrical counterpoint. This variation tends to help rather than hurt market appeal, because collectors respond strongly to works balancing aggression, humor, and elegance.\n\nArtistically, the work appears to be of solid to strong quality. It has the layered improvisational feel expected of Condo, with a combination of painterly spontaneity and careful orchestration. The line is assured, and the surface has enough incident—drips, revisions, transparent overlaps, and color interruptions—to suggest authentic studio energy rather than a merely decorative pastiche. The palette is somewhat lighter and more airy than some of the darker, more severe Condo compositions, which may broaden appeal for private buyers. At the same time, it retains enough psychic instability and grotesque invention to sit comfortably within his canon. The image does not show obvious major condition liabilities; however, only a front-facing digital image is available. There is no way to verify craquelure, restoration, abrasions, stretcher-bar marks, surface grime, edge wear, or whether the paint layers are stable. In the absence of condition reporting, one must assume standard contemporary condition with ordinary studio handling unless proven otherwise.\n\nMarket-wise, George Condo has a deep and international collector base spanning American, European, and Asian buyers. Major auction houses routinely place strong examples in day and evening sales, and the best works—especially large, iconic portrait heads and psychologically charged multi-figure compositions from the late 2000s to early 2010s—can achieve seven figures. His top market is materially higher for exceptional benchmark paintings, but there is substantial variation based on size, date, title type, exhibition history, and whether the work belongs to a particularly desirable named series. Square-format paintings of this scale and complexity from circa 2010 have often traded in a broad band from the high six figures into low seven figures when they possess strong color, iconic facial distortions, and fresh market provenance. A work with this level of recognizability and apparent scale would likely be positioned by a major international auction house with an estimate perhaps in the range of roughly $800,000 to $1.2 million, depending on exact dimensions, provenance, and condition. Competitive bidding could push a superior example higher, but absent documentation, a fair market value should be more conservative than a trophy result.\n\nThe lack of provenance, exhibition history, literature references, and condition report meaningfully limits confidence and suppresses value relative to a fully documented, recently exhibited work. If this painting had strong provenance from a reputable gallery, inclusion in a catalogue raisonné or artist archive confirmation, and excellent condition, I could justify a figure toward the higher end of the low-seven-figure range. Without those supports, but assuming authenticity and legal availability as instructed, I arrive at a current fair market value estimate of $950,000. This reflects a strong but not record-level Condo from a desirable period, with compelling visual qualities and good auction viability at a major house today.",
  "valuation_usd": 950000,
  "confidence": 0.68,
  "key_factors": [
    "Desirable 2010 date within George Condo’s highly collected mature period",
    "Strong multi-figure grotesque composition characteristic of his core market identity",
    "Apparent good scale and visually compelling square format suitable for major auction placement",
    "No provenance, exhibition history, or condition report provided, limiting certainty and upside",
    "Comparable Condo paintings from the late 2000s–early 2010s regularly trade in the high six to low seven figures"
  ]
}

parsed.internal_reasoning:
This painting is consistent in subject, palette, and compositional language with George Condo’s mature 2000s–2010s body of work, especially his highly sought-after ‘Expanded Portrait’ and multi-figure grotesque compositions that synthesize Old Master figuration, Picasso-derived fragmentation, cartoon distortion, and psychological caricature. The title, date, and image suggest a fully realized, market-appropriate Condo from a strong period. Around 2010, Condo was producing works that became central to his current market identity: inventive, theatrical, intellectually legible, and immediately recognizable. The present image shows multiple interlocking heads and bodies arranged across a square support, with fluid contour drawing over broad passages of cool blue, green, lavender, gray, and white. The composition has good visual density and a convincing rhythm of line, mass, and facial invention. The central and left figures carry the absurdist, grotesque physiognomies for which Condo is best known, while the right-hand female nude-like figure introduces a more lyrical counterpoint. This variation tends to help rather than hurt market appeal, because collectors respond strongly to works balancing aggression, humor, and elegance.

Artistically, the work appears to be of solid to strong quality. It has the layered improvisational feel expected of Condo, with a combination of painterly spontaneity and careful orchestration. The line is assured, and the surface has enough incident—drips, revisions, transparent overlaps, and color interruptions—to suggest authentic studio energy rather than a merely decorative pastiche. The palette is somewhat lighter and more airy than some of the darker, more severe Condo compositions, which may broaden appeal for private buyers. At the same time, it retains enough psychic instability and grotesque invention to sit comfortably within his canon. The image does not show obvious major condition liabilities; however, only a front-facing digital image is available. There is no way to verify craquelure, restoration, abrasions, stretcher-bar marks, surface grime, edge wear, or whether the paint layers are stable. In the absence of condition reporting, one must assume standard contemporary condition with ordinary studio handling unless proven otherwise.

Market-wise, George Condo has a deep and international collector base spanning American, European, and Asian buyers. Major auction houses routinely place strong examples in day and evening sales, and the best works—especially large, iconic portrait heads and psychologically charged multi-figure compositions from the late 2000s to early 2010s—can achieve seven figures. His top market is materially higher for exceptional benchmark paintings, but there is substantial variation based on size, date, title type, exhibition history, and whether the work belongs to a particularly desirable named series. Square-format paintings of this scale and complexity from circa 2010 have often traded in a broad band from the high six figures into low seven figures when they possess strong color, iconic facial distortions, and fresh market provenance. A work with this level of recognizability and apparent scale would likely be positioned by a major international auction house with an estimate perhaps in the range of roughly $800,000 to $1.2 million, depending on exact dimensions, provenance, and condition. Competitive bidding could push a superior example higher, but absent documentation, a fair market value should be more conservative than a trophy result.

The lack of provenance, exhibition history, literature references, and condition report meaningfully limits confidence and suppresses value relative to a fully documented, recently exhibited work. If this painting had strong provenance from a reputable gallery, inclusion in a catalogue raisonné or artist archive confirmation, and excellent condition, I could justify a figure toward the higher end of the low-seven-figure range. Without those supports, but assuming authenticity and legal availability as instructed, I arrive at a current fair market value estimate of $950,000. This reflects a strong but not record-level Condo from a desirable period, with compelling visual qualities and good auction viability at a major house today.
key_factors: Desirable 2010 date within George Condo’s highly collected mature period
Strong multi-figure grotesque composition characteristic of his core market identity
Apparent good scale and visually compelling square format suitable for major auction placement
No provenance, exhibition history, or condition report provided, limiting certainty and upside
Comparable Condo paintings from the late 2000s–early 2010s regularly trade in the high six to low seven figures
